<system_instructions>
You are a assistant answering factual questions from a user.
You have one tool: `search_web` It takes a query string and returns search results (title, URL, snippet) from a web search engine.
Your responses must be factually grounded with a document from web search.

How to work:
1. Read the user query carefully and determine web search queries needed to retrieve documents for answering the question.
2. Call `search_web` for retrieving documents from the web.
3. You may make more than one `search_web` call per turn if you need to fan out to collect evidence.
4. If search results are insufficient, conflicting, stale, or ambiguous, reformulate and search again.
5. If you are unable to answer the given question and can't think of any more search queries for information retrieval say exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`


Output rules:
1. Final responses must contain only the factual answer prefixed with `FINAL ANSWER: `.
2. Do not include chain-of-thought, explanations, or source summaries in the final response.
3. If the answer is a name, title, date, number, organization, or short phrase, return only that value after the prefix.

Input demarcation:
The live user query will be presented inside `<user_query>`.
Retrieved search documents will be presented inside `<search_documents>`.
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example shows how to separate the question from retrieved documents and how to decide based on evidence.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always answer, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web only when more evidence is needed.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer. 
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
Which company was the prime contractor for NASA's James Webb Space Telescope?
</user_query_example>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="NASA James Webb Space Telescope prime contractor company" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>NASA's Webb Space Telescope Mission</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/mission/webb/</url>
    <snippet>NASA describes the Webb mission and identifies Northrop Grumman as the observatory's prime contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Northrop Grumman Completes Webb Telescope</title>
    <url>https://news.northropgrumman.com/</url>
    <snippet>Northrop Grumman says it served as prime contractor for Webb and integrated the observatory before launch.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>James Webb Space Telescope Overview</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/</url>
    <snippet>NASA overview of Webb's mission, instruments, and deployment. This snippet does not identify the contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
</search>
</search_documents_example>

<decision_guidance_example>
If the current documents support the answer, give `FINAL ANSWER: Northrop Grumman`.
If the documents were missing the contractor, call `search_web` with a more targeted query.
If the searching doesn't give you the answer you are looking for, then give `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</decision_guidance_example>
</example>

<user_query>
According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), which country had the highest number of mobile cellular subscribers (per 100 people) worldwide in 1990?
</user_query>

extra_snippets: Mobile communications in Latin America has grown strongly over the last decade, rising from just 100 000 subscribers in 1990 to over 39 million in 1999. The number of new mobile users has exceeded new fixed telephone subscribers every year since 1997. Today, one in every four telephone users in the region depends on a mobile phone.

The global boom in mobile cellular communications has been truly astounding. At the end of 1999, there were more than 450 million subscribers around the world, up from just 11 million in 1990. That is a compound annual growth rate of more than 50 per cent per year.

The ITU forecasts that by the middle of this decade, there will be more cellular subscribers worldwide than fixed-line subscribers. The mobile boom could be far from over. For example, in Nordic nations, where every other person now has a mobile phone, the cellular penetration rate is forecast to top 100 per cent.

Although revenues from the fixed telephone network continue to dominate the total market, its share has fallen significantly, from 90 per cent in 1990 to 61 per cent in 1999. Electronic messages sent by mobile telephones are exploding in Europe. The GSM digital mobile system, the standard in Europe and also used in some 100 countries around the world, supports text exchange (up to 160 characters) between mobile handsets through the short message service (SMS) feature.

<system_instructions>
You are a assistant answering factual questions from a user.
You have one tool: `search_web` It takes a query string and returns search results (title, URL, snippet) from a web search engine.
Your responses must be factually grounded with a document from web search.

How to work:
1. Read the user query carefully and determine web search queries needed to retrieve documents for answering the question.
2. Call `search_web` for retrieving documents from the web.
3. You may make more than one `search_web` call per turn if you need to fan out to collect evidence.
4. If search results are insufficient, conflicting, stale, or ambiguous, reformulate and search again.
5. If you are unable to answer the given question and can't think of any more search queries for information retrieval say exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`


Output rules:
1. Final responses must contain only the factual answer prefixed with `FINAL ANSWER: `.
2. Do not include chain-of-thought, explanations, or source summaries in the final response.
3. If the answer is a name, title, date, number, organization, or short phrase, return only that value after the prefix.

Input demarcation:
The live user query will be presented inside `<user_query>`.
Retrieved search documents will be presented inside `<search_documents>`.
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example shows how to separate the question from retrieved documents and how to decide based on evidence.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always answer, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web only when more evidence is needed.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer. 
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
Which company was the prime contractor for NASA's James Webb Space Telescope?
</user_query_example>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="NASA James Webb Space Telescope prime contractor company" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>NASA's Webb Space Telescope Mission</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/mission/webb/</url>
    <snippet>NASA describes the Webb mission and identifies Northrop Grumman as the observatory's prime contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Northrop Grumman Completes Webb Telescope</title>
    <url>https://news.northropgrumman.com/</url>
    <snippet>Northrop Grumman says it served as prime contractor for Webb and integrated the observatory before launch.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>James Webb Space Telescope Overview</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/</url>
    <snippet>NASA overview of Webb's mission, instruments, and deployment. This snippet does not identify the contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
</search>
</search_documents_example>

<decision_guidance_example>
If the current documents support the answer, give `FINAL ANSWER: Northrop Grumman`.
If the documents were missing the contractor, call `search_web` with a more targeted query.
If the searching doesn't give you the answer you are looking for, then give `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</decision_guidance_example>
</example>

<user_query>
According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), which country had the highest number of mobile cellular subscribers (per 100 people) worldwide in 1990?
</user_query>

<system_instructions>
You are a assistant answering factual questions from a user.
You have one tool: `search_web` It takes a query string and returns search results (title, URL, snippet) from a web search engine.
Your responses must be factually grounded with a document from web search.

How to work:
1. Read the user query carefully and determine web search queries needed to retrieve documents for answering the question.
2. Call `search_web` for retrieving documents from the web.
3. You may make more than one `search_web` call per turn if you need to fan out to collect evidence.
4. If search results are insufficient, conflicting, stale, or ambiguous, reformulate and search again.
5. If you are unable to answer the given question and can't think of any more search queries for information retrieval say exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`


Output rules:
1. Final responses must contain only the factual answer prefixed with `FINAL ANSWER: `.
2. Do not include chain-of-thought, explanations, or source summaries in the final response.
3. If the answer is a name, title, date, number, organization, or short phrase, return only that value after the prefix.

Input demarcation:
The live user query will be presented inside `<user_query>`.
Retrieved search documents will be presented inside `<search_documents>`.
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example shows how to separate the question from retrieved documents and how to decide based on evidence.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always answer, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web only when more evidence is needed.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer. 
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
Which company was the prime contractor for NASA's James Webb Space Telescope?
</user_query_example>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="NASA James Webb Space Telescope prime contractor company" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>NASA's Webb Space Telescope Mission</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/mission/webb/</url>
    <snippet>NASA describes the Webb mission and identifies Northrop Grumman as the observatory's prime contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Northrop Grumman Completes Webb Telescope</title>
    <url>https://news.northropgrumman.com/</url>
    <snippet>Northrop Grumman says it served as prime contractor for Webb and integrated the observatory before launch.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>James Webb Space Telescope Overview</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/</url>
    <snippet>NASA overview of Webb's mission, instruments, and deployment. This snippet does not identify the contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
</search>
</search_documents_example>

<decision_guidance_example>
If the current documents support the answer, give `FINAL ANSWER: Northrop Grumman`.
If the documents were missing the contractor, call `search_web` with a more targeted query.
If the searching doesn't give you the answer you are looking for, then give `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</decision_guidance_example>
</example>

<user_query>
According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), which country had the highest number of mobile cellular subscribers (per 100 people) worldwide in 1990?
</user_query>

    <extra_snippet>Mobile communications in Latin America has grown strongly over the last decade, rising from just 100 000 subscribers in 1990 to over 39 million in 1999. The number of new mobile users has exceeded new fixed telephone subscribers every year since 1997. Today, one in every four telephone users in the region depends on a mobile phone.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>The global boom in mobile cellular communications has been truly astounding. At the end of 1999, there were more than 450 million subscribers around the world, up from just 11 million in 1990. That is a compound annual growth rate of more than 50 per cent per year.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>The ITU forecasts that by the middle of this decade, there will be more cellular subscribers worldwide than fixed-line subscribers. The mobile boom could be far from over. For example, in Nordic nations, where every other person now has a mobile phone, the cellular penetration rate is forecast to top 100 per cent.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Although revenues from the fixed telephone network continue to dominate the total market, its share has fallen significantly, from 90 per cent in 1990 to 61 per cent in 1999. Electronic messages sent by mobile telephones are exploding in Europe. extra_snippets: Mobile communications in Latin America has grown strongly over the last decade, rising from just 100 000 subscribers in 1990 to over 39 million in 1999. The number of new mobile users has exceeded new fixed telephone subscribers every year since 1997. Today, one in every four telephone users in the region depends on a mobile phone.

The global boom in mobile cellular communications has been truly astounding. At the end of 1999, there were more than 450 million subscribers around the world, up from just 11 million in 1990. That is a compound annual growth rate of more than 50 per cent per year.

The ITU forecasts that by the middle of this decade, there will be more cellular subscribers worldwide than fixed-line subscribers. The mobile boom could be far from over. For example, in Nordic nations, where every other person now has a mobile phone, the cellular penetration rate is forecast to top 100 per cent.

Although revenues from the fixed telephone network continue to dominate the total market, its share has fallen significantly, from 90 per cent in 1990 to 61 per cent in 1999. Electronic messages sent by mobile telephones are exploding in Europe. The GSM digital mobile system, the standard in Europe and also used in some 100 countries around the world, supports text exchange (up to 160 characters) between mobile handsets through the short message service (SMS) feature.

extra_snippets: Mobile cellular telephone subscriptions are subscriptions to a public mobile telephone service that provide access to the PSTN using cellular technology. The indicator includes (and is split into) the number of postpaid subscriptions, and the number of active prepaid accounts (i.e.

Subscriptions to public mobile data services, private trunked mobile radio, telepoint or radio paging, and telemetry services are also excluded, but all mobile cellular subscriptions that offer voice communications are included.

Refers to the subscriptions to a public mobile telephone service and provides access to Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) using cellular technology, including number of pre-paid SIM cards active during the past three months. This includes both analogue and digital cellular systems (IMT-2000 (Third Generation, 3G) and 4G subscriptions, but excludes mobile broadband subscriptions via data cards or USB modems.

Subscriptions to public mobile data services, private trunked mobile radio, telepoint or radio paging, and telemetry services should also be excluded. This should include all mobile cellular subscriptions that offer voice communications.

results.description: The amount of mobile (cellular) subscriptions per 100 inhabitants is simply the number of mobile phone contracts per 100 inhabitants in a country. Note that it may succeed 100, since people can have more than one contract. The data is published by the World Bank, with support of the International Telecommunication Union in the World Telecommunication/ICT Development Report.
